strands.title: Tensor Projection Implementation: File Size Constraint Encountered
strands.content: CYCLE 38 reached log format discovery. CYCLE 39 attempts to read field structure from proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl but encounters hard constraint: file is 62,325 tokens (~240KB+), exceeding single-read limit of 25,000 tokens. Read with limit=30 also fails, suggesting limit parameter is ignored or file is compressed/represented in token count that exceeds limit. CRITICAL DISCOVERY: The log file is LARGE—likely 1000+ JSONL lines. Standard 'read file sequentially' approach now fails. CONSTRAINT IMPLICATIONS: (1) Cannot load entire file. (2) Cannot use naive line-by-line sequential read in single tool invocation. (3) Must use OFFSET-based chunked reading or search-based sampling. (4) Offset reads enable windowing: read [0:25k], [25k:50k], etc. (5) Search-based approach: grep/jq to extract specific patterns without loading full file. REVISED STRATEGY: Use offset+limit to read first chunk (offset=0, limit=25000), extract field structure from first few JSONL lines within that chunk. Then design pipeline to process file in chunks. This unblocks MVP implementation.

key_claims.text: proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl is 62,325 tokens. Exceeds single read limit (25,000). Direct sequential parse impossible in single operation.
Offset-based chunked reading (offset=0, limit=25000, then offset=25000, limit=25000, etc.) can bypass file size limit. Pipeline processes log in 25KB windows.
62,325 tokens suggests ~1000-2000+ JSONL lines (assuming ~30-60 tokens per turn on average). Baseline_run1 is substantial conversation log.

strands.title: Log File Field Structure: Deferred to Offset-Read Phase
strands.content: CYCLE 38 planned direct read of first JSONL line to inspect fields. CYCLE 39 reveals that direct read is blocked by file size. NEW PLAN: Use offset=0, limit=25000 to read first 25KB chunk. Within that chunk, first few JSONL lines will be complete. Parse those lines to extract field names (role, content, timestamp, metadata, etc.). This approach works: chunk contains full JSON objects at start, even if it's truncated mid-file. Field structure discovery is DEFERRED but UNBLOCKED by offset strategy.

key_claims.text: Offset-based read with limit=25000 will return first 25KB of file. First few JSONL lines will be complete JSON objects. Field structure can be extracted from this chunk.

strands.title: Extraction Pipeline: Chunked Processing Model Required
strands.content: CYCLE 38 designed abstract pipeline assuming full-file load. CYCLE 39 constrains pipeline to chunked mode. NEW ARCHITECTURE: (1) CHUNKING LAYER: Read file in 25KB offset windows. Track chunk boundaries. (2) JSONL LINE BUFFERING: Within each chunk, identify complete JSONL lines (ends with newline). Incomplete lines at chunk boundary are held in buffer for next chunk. (3) TURN PROCESSING: Each complete JSONL line is one turn/event. Process turn → extract claims → cluster strands → update tensor. (4) LOSS TRACKING: Incomplete lines at chunk boundary must be declared as loss until retrieved from next chunk. (5) STREAMING TENSOR UPDATES: After each chunk, emit intermediate tensor state. Final tensor after all chunks processed is ground truth. This model is MORE ROBUST than full-file load: handles memory constraints, enables progressive tensor updates, allows interrupt/resume. IMPLEMENTATION PRIORITY: Implement chunked JSONL parser as core extraction engine.

key_claims.text: Chunked JSONL parsing (offset windows + line buffering) is feasible and necessary. Enables processing of arbitrarily large logs.
Intermediate tensor updates after each chunk enable progressive state tracking and allow pipeline interruption/resumption.

strands.title: Critical Path: Field Structure Discovery → Chunked Parse → Epistemic Assignment → Validation
strands.content: CYCLE 37-38 convergence planned: Design → Format discovery → Implementation. CYCLE 39 reveals constraint that changes implementation model but NOT the critical path. CURRENT STATUS: (A) Format is confirmed (JSONL). (B) Field structure (role, content, timestamp, etc.) still UNKNOWN. (C) Chunked parsing model is NOW required. (D) Epistemic heuristics still need linguistic marker discovery. (E) Validation against hand-annotation still required. BOTTLENECK: Field structure discovery must happen in next cycle. This unblocks all downstream steps. If field names and structure are known, chunked parser can be implemented, and full baseline_run1 can be processed. DEPENDENCY CHAIN: Field structure → Chunked parser impl → Full log process → Claim extraction → Strand clustering → Epistemic assignment → Validation.

key_claims.text: Field structure discovery is critical blocker. Must be resolved in Cycle 40 via offset=0 read. Enables all downstream MVP implementation.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Direct full-file read of JSONL. Planned for Cycle 39 to read entire proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl in one operation.
declared_losses.why: File token count (62,325) exceeds read limit (25,000). Even with limit=30 parameter, file size constraint persists. Single-operation full read is infeasible.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Exact field structure of JSONL entries. Expected to be extracted from first JSONL line in Cycle 39.
declared_losses.why: File size prevents direct read. Deferred to Cycle 40. Field structure discovery will use offset-based chunked read.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: First-pass turn count and file size (wc -l, ls -lh commands). Planned for Cycle 39 immediate actions.
declared_losses.why: Prioritized full-file read first to validate field structure. File size constraint redirects strategy. Metadata commands now secondary to field structure discovery.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Knowledge of whether limit parameter applies to token count or byte count. When limit=30, did it fail due to token count, byte count, or other reason?
declared_losses.why: Tool error message is ambiguous. 'limit' semantics not explicitly defined. Assumed to be token-based, but could be byte-based or other. Affects offset strategy design.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Assumption that JSONL lines are short (10-50 tokens each). If lines are 100+ tokens, 25KB chunk may contain only 250-2500 lines, changing processing efficiency.
declared_losses.why: Haven't inspected real turn content yet. Estimate is rough. Will be refined once first chunk is read.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Potential for single-operation MVP validation. Cycle 39 hoped to process baseline_run1 in one sweep. Now requires iterative chunked processing.
declared_losses.why: File size constraint necessitates multi-cycle chunked approach. Adds complexity and time to validation cycle.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Details of how offset parameter works. Does offset=25000 mean byte offset, token offset, or character offset? How does it interact with JSONL line boundaries?
declared_losses.why: File system semantics not clarified. Offset strategy assumes byte-based offsets, but tool may use token-based. Will be tested in Cycle 40.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Verification that limit=25000 actually returns valid JSONL (complete lines, no mid-JSON truncation). Error message doesn't confirm chunk validity.
declared_losses.why: Tool only reported token count exceeded. Didn't show whether offset+limit returns valid JSONL. Will be verified when offset read is executed.

open_questions: What are the exact field names in each JSONL line? (role, content, timestamp, metadata, etc.) — CRITICAL BLOCKER
Does offset=0, limit=25000 succeed in returning a valid 25KB chunk of JSONL? Will first few lines be complete JSON objects?
How many complete JSONL lines fit in a 25KB chunk? (affects window size strategy)
What is the semantics of the limit parameter? Token count, byte count, or character count? Affects offset strategy design.
Does the tool support multiple consecutive offset reads to reconstruct full file? (offset=0; offset=25000; offset=50000; ...)
Are there nested objects in the JSONL entries? (e.g., message.content vs. flat content field) — needed for parser design
Do timestamps exist in each entry? If so, what format? (ISO 8601, Unix epoch, custom?) — affects turn sequencing
Is there a 'role' field distinguishing user vs. assistant turns? Or other speaker indicators? — needed for claim extraction context
Are there metadata fields like 'turn_id', 'session_id', 'model_name', 'token_count', 'finish_reason'? — affects epistemic assignment
What does the session/d5da3c7a-3c3c-4047-a4ee-276be57567c1.jsonl file contain? Is it auxiliary or integral? (deferred from Cycle 38)
Are session and logs JSONL files synchronized? (same turns indexed in both?)
Does log entry have 'content' as string or nested structure (e.g., content.type, content.text)?
Are there special characters, escape sequences, or JSON encoding artifacts in the content field?
What is the typical turn length (token count or character count)? Affects claim extraction granularity.
Do some turns contain structured data (code blocks, JSON, tables)? How are they formatted?
Is there any error/exception logging in the JSONL file (finish_reason='stop' vs. 'error')?
What does the timestamp range tell us about conversation duration? (E.g., 2026-03-02 suggests future-dated synthetic data?)
First turn—is it a system prompt, user message, or both? Affects initial tensor initialization.
Are turns interleaved (user, assistant, user, assistant) or grouped? Affects parsing logic.
Does the JSONL file have a header row or metadata line before turn entries?
Can the file be parsed as valid JSONL (each line is a complete JSON object)? Any line breaks within JSON?
Are there any null/empty content fields? How should they be handled?
What is the proxy source? (Claude API, custom interceptor, chat interface export?) — affects data provenance
Does baseline_run1/config.json specify the prompt, model, temperature, and other hyperparameters? — CRITICAL for interpreting results
How does the token count (62,325) translate to estimated line count? (rough estimate: 30-60 tokens/line = 1000-2000 lines?)
Will offset-based chunked reading allow complete reconstruction of strand trajectory? Or does chunking lose context across boundaries?
